text: The surgeon performs a dissection to separate the small bowel adhesions from the pelvic region, which are present due to the patient's prior hysterectomy.
The surgeon performs a pelvic lymph node dissection, revealing the small bowel adhered laterally within the pelvic region.

The surgeon identifies the gonadal vessels on the right side, which have been medialized. Initially, there was uncertainty about whether the structure was vaginal mesh, but it is confirmed to be the gonadal vessels.

A sponge is placed to hold the small bowel aside, and the surgeon begins a medial to lateral dissection. The patient has benign disease, and the surgeon plans a transrectal extraction of the specimen, using four eight-millimeter ports to minimize post-operative pain.

The surgeon dissects around the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle, revealing the bifurcation where the left colic artery branches off. The left colic artery is preserved while the superior hemorrhoidal artery is taken. The dissection continues from medial to lateral.

The surgeon places a sponge to protect the retroperitoneum and begins the lateral dissection, marking the planned proximal margin on the bowel, which appears normal. The dissection follows the white line to meet the medial dissection plane.
The surgeon demonstrates a portion of the lateral dissection, focusing on the separation of tissue planes along the lateral aspect of the colon.

The surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure, selectively demonstrating this step. The remaining pelvic adhesions, resulting from diverticular disease and a previous hysterectomy, are being addressed. The surgeon marks the planned distal margin on the bowel.

The surgeon assesses the reach of the surgical instruments, confirming they extend deep past the promontory. The proximal margin is identified, and the mesentery is taken up to this margin while preserving the left colic artery.
The surgeon cleans the distal margin of the colon, ensuring clear delineation of the surgical boundary.
The surgeon checks perfusion using indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence at the margin and proceeds to transect the tissue with scissors. The distal margin is then checked and divided.

The surgeon separates the mesentery from the colon specimen to facilitate transrectal extraction. The colon and mesentery are removed as two separate specimens, with instruments used to hold open the rectum for easier extraction.

The surgeon repositions the colon and begins sewing a two-layer end-to-end anastomosis, using a back row of two LPDS sutures. The sutures are placed approximately five millimeters from the cut edge to achieve inversion of the posterior lip. The surgeon checks ICG to ensure satisfactory perfusion.
The surgeon begins sewing the inner layer of the anastomosis, starting in the middle and sewing out to the patient's left using nine-inch 3-0 V-Loc sutures.

The surgeon begins suturing the anterior inner row of the anastomosis, taking care not to close over the lumen to maintain visibility of the midline for the next stitch. The suture is placed in the midline and extends to the patient's right.

The surgeon transitions to a canal stitch at the near corner, joining two sutures in the midline by alternating between them until the tissue is fully inverted towards the center. The sutures are tied together, followed by an anterior outer layer using PDS sutures.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the anastomosis using a 19mm proctoscope, observing no tension or bubbles, indicating a successful seal.
